1.synthesis: Jacob sets out from Canaan toward Egypt with all he has. At Beersheba — the southernmost point of the promised land, historically sacred to the patriarchs — he offers sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac (v.1). God speaks to him in the night visions: 'Jacob, Jacob' — the double-name address used at every high-stakes divine encounter in Genesis (Genesis 22:11: 'Abraham, Abraham'; Exodus 3:4: 'Moses, Moses'). The divine assurance is threefold: 'I am God, the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for there I will make you into a great nation. I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I will also bring you up again' (vv.3–4). Joseph's hand will close his eyes. Jacob rises and continues the journey with his entire household (vv.5–7).
1.historical_context: Beersheba is the threshold of the promised land: to cross it southward is to leave Canaan. Abraham had made a covenant with Abimelech here (21:33), Isaac had received a theophany here after conflict with the Philistines (26:23–25), and Jacob had passed through it on his flight to Paddan-Aram. The Beersheba theophany is thus the fifth patriarchal theophany at this specific site, making it the covenant boundary marker par excellence. The divine fear-not formula ('do not be afraid to go down') implies that the descent was religiously troubling: Egypt was not the promised land; going there risked the covenant family's absorption into foreign culture. But God declares himself not a territorial deity: 'I myself will go down with you' — YHWH will descend to Egypt, remain Israel's God there, and bring the family back.
1.literary_structure: The theophany at Beersheba is the last direct divine speech to any of the patriarchs before the Exodus. After this, God will not speak again until the burning bush (Exodus 3). The promise 'I myself will go down with you' (v.4) and 'I will also bring you up again' creates the structural frame for the entire Exodus narrative: what begins here in Genesis 46 with a divinely-sanctioned descent will be completed in Exodus 12 with a divinely-orchestrated ascent. The sacrifices Jacob offers at Beersheba (v.1) — 'sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac' — honor the covenant continuity: the same God who spoke to Isaac at Beersheba now speaks to Jacob there, sending him onward into Egypt with the same promise-structure.
1.theological_application: Gill on the divine assurance: 'I will go down with thee into Egypt; which was enough to silence all his fears; for if the presence of God went with him to protect and defend, to bless and prosper him, and to direct, support, and comfort, he had nothing to fear from any quarter.' Matthew Henry: 'Beersheba was a place where God had appeared to Abraham … and Isaac … and it was fit that Jacob should there be encouraged by a fresh appearance of God, and a repetition of the covenant.' The pattern of divine accompaniment — 'I will go with you' — is the foundational covenant assurance that runs through all Scripture, culminating in Christ's 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20). God does not send his people into hard places alone.
1.christology: The Beersheba theophany gives the Exodus its theological framework: the descent into Egypt is not divine abandonment but divine accompaniment. God himself goes down. The pattern of voluntary descent for the sake of rescue is the proto-incarnational logic of the entire Exodus: God descends to where his people are suffering, is present with them in the darkness of slavery, and brings them out. Paul in Philippians 2:7–8 describes Christ who 'emptied himself … coming in the likeness of men … humbling himself … death on a cross' as the ultimate enactment of this descent. The word to Jacob — 'I will also bring you up again' — is the promise of resurrection in its proto-form: the descent does not end in Egypt.

8.pericope_label: Jacob's Family Who Came to Egypt
8.synthesis: The genealogical register of Jacob's family who came to Egypt (vv.8–27) lists seventy persons in all — the complete household of Israel entering Egypt. The list is organized by mother: Leah's descendants (vv.8–15, 33 persons), Zilpah's descendants (vv.16–18, 16 persons), Rachel's descendants (vv.19–22, 14 persons), Bilhah's descendants (vv.23–25, 7 persons), with the total of 70 given in v.27. The number 70 is theologically significant: it is the number of the nations in the Table of Nations (Genesis 10), echoing the promise that Jacob's family would become a counterpart to all the nations of the earth. Joseph and his two sons, already in Egypt, are counted within Rachel's 14 but noted separately. The list includes Er and Onan (who died in Canaan) and great-grandchildren already born, suggesting the number is a symbolic completeness rather than a literal head-count at the moment of departure.
8.historical_context: The number 70 for the total of Jacob's household entering Egypt is a canonical figure that recurs in Exodus 1:5, Deuteronomy 10:22, and Acts 7:14 (where Stephen, following the LXX, gives 75). The discrepancy between MT (70) and LXX/Acts (75) reflects different methods of counting Joseph's Egyptian-born grandchildren. Seventy is the number of completeness in the ANE: 70 elders of Israel (Exodus 24:1), 70 years of exile (Jeremiah 29:10). The list's inclusion of deceased persons (Er, Onan) and those not yet born at the time of departure suggests it is a tribal-genealogical inventory rather than a departure census. Deuteronomy 32:8's 'when the Most High gave the nations their inheritance … he fixed the borders of the peoples according to the number of the sons of God' may allude to this 70 as the foundation of Israel's place among the 70 nations.
8.literary_structure: The genealogical list interrupts the narrative flow of the journey to Egypt, serving a theological rather than dramatic purpose: to document the complete household of Israel that will become the nation. The list is structured matrilineally — each wife's or concubine's descendants grouped together — maintaining the family structure established in the birth narratives of chs.29–30. The ending summary (v.27: 'all the persons of the house of Jacob who came into Egypt were seventy') creates a formal closure that the Exodus will open: Exodus 1:5 references this same count as the starting point ('the descendants of Jacob were seventy persons in all; Joseph was already in Egypt'), and the narrative proceeds from 70 to a multitude too great to count.
8.theological_application: Gill on the number 70: 'all the souls that came with Jacob into Egypt … the number of them were threescore and ten, or seventy, including Jacob himself, and Joseph and his two sons … this was a small number … compared with those of whom their posterity consisted; but such is the wonderful increase of God's people when he is pleased to bless them.' Matthew Henry: 'The fulfillment of the divine promise is noted here: of seventy persons, God would make the nation that would eventually number in the millions. Providence uses small beginnings.' The 70 who enter Egypt will become the nation that Exodus 12 counts at 600,000 men — an increase that required four centuries and the specific conditions of an oppressive Egypt that paradoxically drove the multiplication.
8.christology: The family of Jacob entering Egypt as 70 persons corresponds to the 70 nations of Genesis 10, making Israel the covenant counterpart of all the peoples of the earth. The mission of the 70 (or 72) disciples in Luke 10:1 — sent ahead of Jesus to every town he intended to visit — carries this numerical echo: the new Israel is sent to the nations that Jacob's 70 mirror. The small beginning in Egypt and the vast multiplication that follows typify the mustard seed pattern of the kingdom: 'the smallest of all seeds … becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and make nests in its branches' (Matthew 13:32).

28.synthesis: Jacob sends Judah ahead to direct the way to Goshen (v.28) — Judah, who two chapters earlier offered himself as a slave in Egypt, now leads the family to Egypt's best region. Joseph harnesses his chariot and goes up to meet his father in Goshen (v.29). The reunion is described with restrained power: 'He presented himself to him and fell on his neck and wept on his neck a good while.' Jacob's response is the NT's model of the satisfied death: 'Now let me die, since I have seen your face and know that you are still alive' (v.30). Joseph then coaches his family on how to present themselves to Pharaoh — as shepherds, so that they will be settled in Goshen, which Egyptians will not share with livestock keepers (vv.31–34). The geographical-cultural antipathy between Egyptian agriculture and pastoral nomadism becomes the providential mechanism of the family's preservation of distinct identity.
28.historical_context: Goshen (Egyptian Kesem) is in the eastern Nile Delta, suitable for grazing and at some remove from the Egyptian cultural and religious center. The note that 'all shepherds are an abomination to the Egyptians' (v.34) reflects Egyptian literary sources: Egyptian texts express aristocratic disdain for nomadic pastoralists, the ʿAmw (Asiatics), who were associated with the Hyksos period of foreign domination. This cultural distinction serves God's purposes: the Israelite family is kept separate from full assimilation into Egyptian life, maintaining its own identity through the cattle-keeping that marks them as culturally other. The instruction to call themselves 'men of livestock from our youth' (v.34) is Joseph's providential manipulation of Egyptian prejudice to secure the family's social isolation and, thereby, their survival as a distinct people.
28.literary_structure: The reunion of Jacob and Joseph is the emotional climax of the descent-to-Egypt narrative, corresponding to the earlier reunion of Jacob and Esau (ch.33). In both cases, the feared meeting results in tears and embrace rather than the violence anticipated. Jacob's 'now I can die' (v.30) uses the same language and sentiment as Simeon's in Luke 2:29–30: 'Lord, now you are letting your servant depart in peace … for my eyes have seen your salvation.' The one long mourned and thought dead is revealed alive; the bereaved elder sees him and his life is complete. The practical arrangement for Goshen (vv.31–34) that follows the reunion shows that the narrator does not sentimentalize: the providential isolation of the family in Goshen is equally God's provision as the reunion itself.
28.theological_application: Gill on Jacob's 'now let me die': 'having seen his face, and known that he was alive, he was willing to die when it pleased God, since he had had this sight, which was more than he ever expected to have … the sight of the face of his dear son Joseph … was to him more desirable than life itself.' Matthew Henry: 'Jacob said, now let me die. He had often wished to die in the depth of his sorrow; he now wishes to die in the height of his joy, lest it should be allayed.' The soul's deep satisfaction at seeing the beloved child alive — after years of mourning — is a model of the believer's readiness for death once Christ has been seen. Life is complete when the Lord has been seen. To see his face is worth the entire journey.
28.christology: Jacob's 'now let me die, for I have seen your face' (v.30) is the Simeon pattern: the aged believer who has waited in grief and hope sees the living fulfillment of everything promised and is ready to depart in peace. Simeon in Luke 2:29–30 explicitly identifies seeing the Christ-child as the completion that enables his death: 'my eyes have seen your salvation.' The Egyptian separation strategy — using Egyptian antipathy toward shepherds to preserve Israel's distinctness — is the providential pattern that Paul describes in Romans 11:11: Israel's apparent disadvantage becomes the mechanism of their preservation and of blessing to the nations.
